nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and forest peat swamps with dense leaf litter, submerged roots, and riparian vegetation; highly stained, extremely soft and acidic water with near-zero hardness

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved or floating plants (Java moss, Microsorum, Salvinia)
leaf litter (catappa/Indian almond leaves) for tannins and shelter
driftwood and twisted roots for cover and tannin release
dim lighting or shaded areas via floating plants
dark substrate to enhance coloration and reduce stress
sponge filter or gentle flow only — strong current stresses this species

diet.notes: The Phoenix Rasbora is a micropredator in the wild, feeding on tiny invertebrates, zooplankton, and small insects. In captivity it generally accepts high-quality micro-pellets and fine flake, but tends to thrive with regular live or frozen micro-foods: baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, micro worms, cyclops, and frozen rotifers. Small particle size is essential — standard flake foods may be too large for the tiny mouth. Feed small amounts two to three times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful Betta species (e.g., B. imbellis)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina — adult shrimp safe; shrimp-safe species)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them (most standard community fish)
boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete them for food
fish requiring hard alkaline water (incompatible chemistry)
baby shrimp (fry may be predated by the rasboras themselves)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Andrewbogott. 1302x1357 px. Group of two adults against dark substrate — best represents this schooling species; sharp, showing bronzy-copper body, diffuse dark lateral stripe and red-tipped fins.
Photographer: Andrewbogott. 1043x713 px. Clean full-body lateral view of a single specimen; sharp, clearly shows the bronzy body and thick diffuse dark stripe distinguishing it from Chili Rasbora.
Photographer: Andrewbogott. 1337x731 px. Vibrant male in full colour showing intense red markings and the dark mid-body blotch; sharp, adds coloration variety (caudal fin edge slightly cropped).

summary: The Phoenix Rasbora is a tiny blackwater micro-fish from Borneo's peat swamps, reaching around 2 cm. It resembles the Chili Rasbora but has a bronzy-copper body and a thicker dark lateral stripe. Generally peaceful and shrimp-safe, it does best in soft, acidic, tannin-rich water in groups of eight or more.
careNotes:
The Phoenix Rasbora (Boraras merah) is one of the smallest fish kept in the aquarium hobby, reaching only around 2 cm as an adult. It is endemic to the Jelai Bila river basin of Central Kalimantan, Indonesia, where it inhabits heavily shaded, tannin-stained peat swamp streams with extremely soft, acidic water (pH 4–6.5, near-zero hardness). Replicating these blackwater conditions is key to long-term success: use reverse-osmosis or otherwise very soft source water, add Indian almond leaves or alder cones for tannins, and include driftwood. General hardness and carbonate hardness should both be kept very low; fish kept in even moderately hard water tend to show faded coloration and a shortened lifespan. A well-planted 40–60 L nano aquarium with a sponge filter providing gentle flow is ideal for a colony of 8–12.

This species is frequently mislabeled as the Chili Rasbora (Boraras brigittae) in the trade. The Phoenix Rasbora has a bronzy, coppery body base and a thicker, more diffuse dark lateral stripe, while the Chili Rasbora tends toward a brighter, more saturated red with a thinner and sharper stripe. Both species require similar care.

Phoenix Rasboras are generally safe to keep with adult dwarf shrimp, including Neocaridina and Caridina species, making them a popular choice for planted shrimp tanks. Newly born shrimplets, however, may occasionally be picked off. Keep this species only with tankmates of similarly small size and matching water-chemistry requirements — most standard tropical community fish prefer harder, more alkaline water than this species tolerates. Dim lighting with ample shade provided by floating plants or dense planting is strongly recommended; the fish tends to display its best coloration and lowest stress under subdued light. Groups of at least eight are advisable, as isolated individuals often become shy and may refuse food.
breedingNotes: The Phoenix Rasbora is an egg-scattering, continuous spawner that provides no parental care — adults will readily eat their own eggs and fry if given the opportunity. Condition prospective breeders with live and frozen micro-foods before attempting spawning. A small dedicated breeding tank of 10–20 L with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5, gH 0–2) and a substrate of fine-leaved spawning mops or Java moss works well. Eggs are tiny and adhesive and typically hatch in approximately 48 hours. Remove adults after spawning is observed, or provide sufficiently dense plant cover that fry have a chance to escape. Newly hatched fry are very small and require infusoria or commercial micro-fry foods for the first week before they are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms. Breeding is achievable in well-maintained blackwater setups but is generally considered moderately difficult due to the tiny fry size and their sensitivity to water quality.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boraras merah
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (15–20 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (4.0–6.5), hardness (18–90 ppm / approx. gH 1–5), native range (Jelai Bila river basin, Central Kalimantan, Indonesia), biotope (blackwater peat swamp), diet (micropredator), minimum tank base (45×30 cm), schooling group size (8–10 minimum), breeding method and difficulty. Used as primary authority for care parameters.

additional.name: FishBase — Boraras merah (ID 22603)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.0 cm SL), native range (western and southern Borneo), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, Rasborinae), trophic level 3.1 (consistent with invertivore/micropredator), IUCN status Data Deficient (2019). FishBase lists family as Danionidae (formerly placed in Cyprinidae — reflecting modern molecular phylogeny; Danionidae is the currently accepted family for Boraras).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Chili Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Covers B. brigittae (closest relative / trade substitute for B. merah); confirms shared care requirements: temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0 (trade/pragmatic range), group size 8–12 minimum, diet of frozen rotifers, cyclops, baby brine shrimp, micro worms. Minimum tank noted as 11 L (3 gal) for B. brigittae; 40 L chosen for B. merah per SeriouslyFish 45×30 cm base and to allow adequate group size with filtration.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg-scatterer and continuous spawner that shows no parental care; adults will consume eggs and fry if not separated. Breeding requires very soft, acidic water (pH 5–6.5, gH 0–2), fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, and removal of adults after spawning. Fry are very small and need infusoria or micro-fry foods before they can take baby brine shrimp. Achievable in a dedicated setup but generally considered moderately difficult due to fry fragility and sensitivity to water quality.
